name: Korean Sweet & Spicy Fish
why: A brilliant 20-minute Korean dish that keeps the fish crisp while delivering bold sweet-spicy flavor — the sauce is added after cooking, not during, so the crunch stays intact. Perfect for Martin's ongoing Korean cuisine exploration with a technique he hasn't tried before.
author: Henry
ingredients: 4 dorade grise fillets (or sea bream, snapper, or any firm white fish), skin and bones removed
3-4 tbsp cooking oil (peanut oil works well)

steps: Blend the pear, onion, garlic, and ginger in a food processor until completely smooth.
Add soy sauce, sugar, gochugaru, mirin, sesame oil, black pepper, and sesame seeds. Mix well.
Set the sauce aside while you prepare the fish.
Pat the fish fillets dry with paper towels.
Lightly dust each fillet with all-purpose flour, shaking off any excess.
Optional but recommended: wrap fillets in kombu (dried kelp) and refrigerate overnight for kombu-jime seasoning — this adds deep umami and tenderizes the flesh.
Heat a large skillet over medium-high heat.
Add oil and swirl to coat the pan evenly.
Cook the fillets until golden brown and deeply crispy, about 4-5 minutes per side (8-10 minutes total).
The fish should release easily from the pan when properly seared — don't rush this step.
Transfer the crispy fish to a serving plate.
Spoon a generous amount of the sweet & spicy sauce over each fillet.
If you have a kitchen torch, lightly torch the sauce for extra smoky depth.
Serve immediately with steamed rice, simple sautéed greens, or a light cucumber salad.

pro_tips: If you use kombu-jime (overnight kelp wrapping), skip additional salt — the fish is already seasoned from the kombu brine.
The key technique: sauce goes ON TOP after cooking, never during. This keeps the fish crispy while still delivering bold flavor — a game-changer compared to braised fish dishes.
